Limiting Laws and Counterion Condensation in Polyelectrolyte Solutions II. Self-Diffusion of the Small Ions GERALD S. MANNING * The Rockefeller University, New York, New York 10021

(Received 10 March 1969) The concepts developed in Part I of this series are applied to the self-diffusion of counterions and co-ions. The condensed counterions are assumed to have no mobility, while the uncondensed counterions and all co-ions execute Brownian motion subject to the locally inhomogeneous electric field created by the polyelectrolyte molecules. The polyelectrolyte field is assumed to have small amplitude, in analogy to the Debye-Hiickel treatment of uncondensed mobile ions in Part I. The self-diffusion coefficients of the counterion and the co-ion are both less than the coresponding values in the absence of polyelectrolyte, but the coefficient for the counterion is much less than that for the co-ion. The results for the self-diffusion coefficient of the counterion in a salt-free polyelectrolyte solution are in good agreement with experimental data.

I. INTRODUCTION

with L the total contour length of the polyion in question and P the total number of charged groups on the polyion (i.e., b is the average charge spacing in the fully stretched configuration). In particular, when ~> 1 and all small ions and polyion charged groups are monovalent, it was assumed that the fraction (1-~-1) of the charged groups on the polyion are fully neutralized by condensation of counter ions on the line charge. Thus, if n. is the equivalent polyion concentration and n8 is the concentration of added simple salt, the effective concentration of counterions in the solution is t-1n.+n s• The motivation for this assumption was the observation of Onsager that the configuration integral for a system of line charges and point ions is divergent when the charge density of the line charge is characterized by ~> 1. If ~< 1, on the other hand, condensation does not occur and the effective concentration of counterions is equal to its stoichiometric concentration n.+n8. Finally, on the basis of certain features of various analyses of the Poisson-Boltzmann equation (see Part I for references), the Debye--Hiickel approxi-

When ~< 1, all small ions are subject to Brownian motion. As explained previously,3 the polyions may be assumed to be fixed in space, thereby creating a locally inhomogeneous electric field- Vif; which behaves like an external force on the small ions. In addition, a uniform external force F is imagined to act on each small ion; at the end of the calculation, the resulting mobility is related by Einstein's law to the self-diffusion coefficient. For a mobile ion of Species i and valence Zi (Zi= ±1 throughout, although formal extension of the theory to higher-valence types is straightfOl ward), the following expression holds for the local flux j;:

Here riO is the friction constant of ion i at infinite dilution in the pure solvent, nF i is the local concentration of i as perturbed by the force F, and qW is the dimensionless counterpart to if;F, the local electrostatic potential set up by the fixed polyions: 2 S. Phys. 46, 2324 (1967).

In the first paper of this series,! hereafter referred to as Part I, various colligative properties of a polyelectrolyte solution were calculated on the basis of certain plausible assumptions. Each polyion was represented as an infinitely long line charge, interactions among polyions were neglected, and the solvent was considered to be a continuum whose dielectric constant E was everywhere equal to that of the pure bulk solvent. A certain parameter ~, related to the charge density of the line charge, was assigned a key role:

mation was assumed to be applicable to the uncondensed small ions. As discussed in detail in Part I, there is a sense in which results based on the above assumptions may be regarded as "limiting laws." It is the purpose of the present article to indicate how these assumptions may be used to calculate transport properties of the small ions, especially their selfdiffusion coefficients. Lifson, Jackson, and Coriell2 were the first to treat this problem from a molecular point of view. The present author then extended their calculation to a more realistic model but solved the resulting equation in only a highly approximate way.3 The following theory is intended to remove the deficiencies of the previous work.

This order among the three quantities is confirmed by experiment (see Fig. 5 of Part I). Before concluding, a brief discussion of the assumption expressed by Eq. (37) is in order. That the condensed counterions have no mobility at all is doubtful, for, as has been pointed out by Katchalsky, Alexandrowicz, and Kedem,1 counterions near the polyelectrolyte

The agreement of theory and experiment is good for ~> 1; for lower values of ~ there is reason to believe that a line charge is not a good representation of the polyion configuration.6 In Part I it was found that the osmotic coefficient CPP and the counterion activity coefficient 'YIP were also in good agreement with experiment for ~> 1 but not for smaller values. In Fig. 1 a curve obtained from Eq. (49) is drawn; the points are the data in Table I. Also included as dashed curves are the theoretical expressions derived in Part I (see Fig. 5 there) for CPP and 'YIP. The differences in these curves are of interest because all three quantities-cpp, 'YIP, and (D1PjD10)-are commonly referred to in the literature as measures of ion "binding." The quantities differ in the present theory because the Debye-Htickel interactions of the uncondensed counterions with the polyion contribute differently to each of the three quantities. From Eqs. (41) and (SIb) of Part I and Eq (49) of the present article, the following set of relations is found for ~> 1:

SOLUTIONS. II

Indeed, the enormously high polarizability of a polyelectrolyte solution probably constitutes direct evidence that such is true. 7 The present results suggest that D1(e) is small; nevertheless, the possibility exists that D1(e) should be treated as an adjustable parameter rather than set equal to zero. The need for more data, for both salt-containing and salt-free solutions, is evident. Modification of a previous calculation for the counterion conductivity 4 to take into account the presence of condensed counterions is trivial and does not change the final result that in the absence of added salt the counterion flux J is given by

E is the external electric field and u is the electrophoretic mobility of the polyion, positive for a polycation, negative for a polyanion. Equation (51) is contingent on the assumption that D1(e) = 0 and, since it is in agreement with experimental data,6.8 provides another argument that the assumption is physically realistic.

Angular and Wavelength Dependence of the Light Scattered from a Cloud of Particles Formed by Homogeneous Nucleation GILBERT D. STEIN* Department of Engineering and Applied Science, Yale University, Ne:w Haven, Connecticut (Received 10 February 1969)

Details of the features of light scattering from ice particles formed via condensation by homogeneous nucleation are presented. An argon-ion laser is used, and the results indicate that dipole or Rayleigh scattering is applicable to the situation under study. In addition, measurements of the number and the mean radius of the particles are made.

INTRODUCTION

The motivation for these experiments was the desire to investigate the light scattering in greater detail in order to substantiate the measurements and interpretation of earlier work. l This previous work gave results of the light scattered at 8= 90° by a cloud of ice particles issuing from a supersonic nozzle. The polariza-

tion of the incoming light was perpendicular to the plane of observation (EJ.). The same wind tunnel,2 nozzle, vapor (water), and carrier gas (air) are used in the experiment described here. 3 However the current experiments are made with a pulsed argon-ion laser as the light source. Furthermore, in contrast to the previous studies, the G. D. Stein, Rev. Sci. Instr. 40, 1058 (1959). G. D.
